clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman is admitted with a 5-day history of jaundice, malaise, and right upper quadrant discomfort following a recent paracetamol overdose. On examination she is deeply jaundiced, confused, and has asterixis. Her INR is 6.2, bilirubin 420 µmol/L, ALT 4800 U/L, creatinine 198 µmol/L, and blood glucose 2.9 mmol/L. pH is 7.27 on arterial blood gas. Which single prognostic criterion, if met, most strongly indicates the need for urgent liver transplant listing in this patient?
options: Bilirubin > 300 µmol/L persisting beyond day 7
Arterial pH < 7.30 after adequate resuscitation
ALT > 3000 U/L at presentation
Presence of grade II hepatic encephalopathy
Serum sodium < 130 mmol/L
explanation: In paracetamol-induced acute liver failure, the King's College Criteria are used to identify patients who require urgent transplant listing. A single criterion of arterial pH < 7.30 after adequate resuscitation (irrespective of grade of encephalopathy) is sufficient to meet the criteria and carries a very poor prognosis without transplant. This patient meets this criterion with a pH of 7.27.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Elevated bilirubin is a marker of severity but is not a standalone King's College criterion for paracetamol overdose.
Arterial pH < 7.30 after resuscitation is the correct single criterion — it alone is sufficient to list for transplant in paracetamol-induced ALF.
Peak ALT level at presentation does not form part of the King's College Criteria; the degree of synthetic dysfunction and acidosis are more prognostically relevant.
Grade III–IV encephalopathy (not grade II) is required as part of the combined criterion (along with INR > 6.5 and creatinine > 300 µmol/L) in the King's College Criteria.
Hyponatraemia is a risk factor for cerebral oedema in ALF but is not a standalone King's College transplant criterion.
clinicalPearl: In paracetamol-induced acute liver failure, arterial pH < 7.30 after resuscitation is a single sufficient criterion for urgent transplant listing under the King's College Criteria.
keyClue: pH 7.27 on ABG after presentation with paracetamol overdose and signs of multi-organ dysfunction.
commonExamTrap: Students focus on ALT level or bilirubin as markers of severity rather than recognising that acidosis alone meets transplant criteria.
missedConcept: King's College Criteria for paracetamol-induced acute liver failure and the significance of arterial acidosis as a single sufficient transplant criterion.
redFlagReason: Acute liver failure with acidosis, coagulopathy, and encephalopathy is life-threatening and requires immediate transplant listing — delay worsens mortality.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a 10-year history of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) presents with worsening jaundice, 6 kg weight loss over 3 months, and new-onset pruritus. ERCP shows a dominant stricture at the hepatic hilum. CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 890 U/mL. Brush cytology is inconclusive. Which of the following most accurately describes the prognosis and next step for this patient?
options: Dominant PSC strictures are always benign; repeat ERCP with dilation in 6 weeks
Cholangiocarcinoma is likely; this patient should be urgently assessed for surgical resectability or liver transplant evaluation
Elevated CA 19-9 alone is diagnostic of cholangiocarcinoma; no further imaging is needed before planning palliative care
Start ursodeoxycholic acid at high dose and repeat CA 19-9 in 3 months
Perform colonoscopy first as PSC is strongly associated with colorectal cancer
explanation: PSC carries a lifetime risk of cholangiocarcinoma of approximately 10–15%. A dominant stricture with markedly elevated CA 19-9, constitutional symptoms, and weight loss is highly suspicious for cholangiocarcinoma. Despite inconclusive cytology, urgent cross-sectional imaging (MRCP/CT) and surgical or transplant centre review for resectability assessment is mandatory given the strong clinical suspicion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Dominant strictures in PSC must never be assumed benign, especially with systemic features of malignancy and markedly elevated CA 19-9.
This is correct — the constellation of PSC with a dominant hilar stricture, weight loss, and high CA 19-9 mandates urgent oncological assessment for cholangiocarcinoma.

CA 19-9 is not diagnostic alone; it can be elevated in benign biliary obstruction. However, the overall clinical picture mandates urgent assessment, not an assumption that further staging is unnecessary.
Ursodeoxycholic acid does not treat cholangiocarcinoma; watchful waiting with UDCA would constitute a dangerous delay in a potentially resectable malignancy.
Although PSC is associated with IBD and colorectal cancer, colonoscopy is not the immediate priority when cholangiocarcinoma is suspected — biliary assessment takes precedence.
clinicalPearl: In PSC, a dominant stricture with markedly elevated CA 19-9 and constitutional symptoms should be treated as cholangiocarcinoma until proven otherwise — urgent assessment for resectability is required.
keyClue: Long-standing PSC + dominant hilar stricture + CA 19-9 890 U/mL + weight loss.
commonExamTrap: Assuming inconclusive cytology rules out malignancy — in PSC, biliary cytology has low sensitivity and a high index of clinical suspicion must be maintained.
missedConcept: PSC-associated cholangiocarcinoma: risk factors, clinical presentation, and the diagnostic limitations of brush cytology.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and obesity (BMI 33 kg/m²) was diagnosed with MASLD 5 years ago. He is now found on routine review to have a platelet count of 98 × 10⁹/L, albumin 29 g/L, bilirubin 38 µmol/L, and INR 1.6. Ultrasound shows a coarse liver echotexture with splenomegaly. He feels well and denies alcohol use. Which complication surveillance programme is now most important to initiate or intensify?
options: Annual liver biopsy to track fibrosis progression
Six-monthly liver ultrasound and serum AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance
Two-yearly colonoscopy for colorectal cancer surveillance
Annual 24-hour urine copper collection for Wilson's disease
Three-monthly viral hepatitis serology
explanation: The biochemical and imaging findings (low platelets, low albumin, raised bilirubin, raised INR, splenomegaly) are consistent with MASLD-related cirrhosis. Patients with cirrhosis of any cause, including MASLD, should undergo six-monthly liver ultrasound with AFP measurement as surveillance for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), as this improves early detection and survival. MASLD is now one of the leading causes of HCC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Annual liver biopsy is not standard practice once cirrhosis is established; non-invasive monitoring and complication surveillance take precedence.
Correct — six-monthly ultrasound and AFP is the standard HCC surveillance programme for cirrhotic patients, including those with MASLD-related cirrhosis.
Colonoscopy surveillance is not directly linked to MASLD-related cirrhosis; while metabolic syndrome does increase colorectal risk, HCC surveillance is the immediate priority here.
There is nothing in this presentation to suggest Wilson's disease, which presents in younger patients with neuropsychiatric features, Kayser-Fleischer rings, or haemolysis.
Viral hepatitis serology is a one-time baseline test for patients with liver disease, not a repeated surveillance programme in established MASLD with cirrhosis.
clinicalPearl: MASLD-related cirrhosis carries a significant risk of HCC — all cirrhotic patients require six-monthly liver ultrasound and AFP regardless of aetiology.
keyClue: MASLD with low platelets, low albumin, raised INR, splenomegaly — consistent with cirrhosis, mandating HCC surveillance.
commonExamTrap: Students may focus on managing the underlying metabolic syndrome rather than recognising that established cirrhosis requires active HCC surveillance.
missedConcept: MASLD as a leading cause of HCC; the importance of cirrhosis recognition and six-monthly surveillance.

clinicalStem: A 47-year-old woman with obesity and hypertriglyceridaemia is found to have steatosis on liver ultrasound. She has no symptoms. Her FIB-4 score is calculated at 3.8. ALT is 72 U/L, AST 68 U/L, and platelets are 130 × 10⁹/L. She is a non-drinker. Which statement best describes the significance of this FIB-4 result and the appropriate next step?
options: FIB-4 of 3.8 is reassuring — no further investigation of fibrosis is needed
FIB-4 of 3.8 indicates high risk of advanced fibrosis and warrants referral for specialist assessment including liver stiffness measurement or biopsy
FIB-4 is only validated for alcoholic liver disease; this score cannot be used in MASLD
A FIB-4 of 3.8 is diagnostic of cirrhosis; she should be listed for liver transplantation
Start high-dose ursodeoxycholic acid to lower the FIB-4 score
explanation: FIB-4 is a validated non-invasive fibrosis score used in MASLD. A score > 2.67 is considered high risk for advanced fibrosis (F3–F4) and warrants specialist referral for further assessment, typically with transient elastography (FibroScan) or liver biopsy. This patient's score of 3.8 exceeds the high-risk threshold, making option B the correct next step.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A FIB-4 of 3.8 is above the high-risk threshold of 2.67 and is not reassuring; inaction would be inappropriate.
Correct — FIB-4 > 2.67 indicates high probability of advanced fibrosis and specialist referral is recommended per NICE and EASL guidelines.
FIB-4 is widely validated in MASLD/NAFLD and is in fact a recommended primary screening tool for fibrosis in this condition.

FIB-4 is not diagnostic of cirrhosis; it is a risk stratification tool. Further assessment is needed before making a diagnosis of cirrhosis, and transplant listing would be premature without confirmation.
Ursodeoxycholic acid is not approved for MASLD and does not reduce fibrosis scores; there is no evidence it alters the natural history of MASLD.
clinicalPearl: FIB-4 > 2.67 in MASLD indicates high risk of advanced fibrosis and should prompt specialist referral for transient elastography or biopsy — do not reassure and discharge.
keyClue: FIB-4 score of 3.8 in a patient with steatosis and metabolic risk factors.
commonExamTrap: Confusing FIB-4 as diagnostic rather than a risk stratification tool, or not knowing the high-risk threshold of 2.67.
missedConcept: FIB-4 score interpretation in MASLD and the threshold above which specialist referral is indicated.

clinicalStem: A 61-year-old man with MASLD-related cirrhosis presents with confusion, fever (38.8°C), and increasing abdominal distension. He has known ascites managed with diuretics. Paracentesis yields cloudy fluid: ascitic neutrophil count is 320 cells/mm³. Serum albumin is 22 g/L, ascitic albumin is 9 g/L. Blood cultures are pending. His creatinine, previously normal, is now 148 µmol/L. Which is the most important immediate management step?
options: Start oral ciprofloxacin and review in 48 hours as an outpatient
Administer IV albumin (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis) alongside prompt IV third-generation cephalosporin (e.g. cefotaxime)
Perform repeat paracentesis with large-volume drainage to reduce intraperitoneal pressure before starting antibiotics
Start IV terlipressin alone as the creatinine rise confirms hepatorenal syndrome
Increase diuretic dose and add prophylactic norfloxacin without antibiotics
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is defined by an ascitic neutrophil count ≥ 250 cells/mm³. Immediate treatment is IV third-generation cephalosporin (e.g. cefotaxime 2 g TDS). Critically, IV albumin (1.5 g/kg on day 1, then 1 g/kg on day 3) reduces the risk of hepatorenal syndrome and significantly improves survival — this is a mandatory co-treatment. Delaying antibiotics or giving oral therapy alone is dangerous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are entirely inadequate for SBP — this requires IV antibiotics and hospitalisation, particularly given the rising creatinine.
Correct — IV cefotaxime plus IV albumin is the evidence-based standard of care for SBP; albumin specifically prevents progression to hepatorenal syndrome.
Large-volume paracentesis is contraindicated in active SBP as the immediate priority is infection treatment; drainage before antibiotics would be dangerous.

Terlipressin is used in established hepatorenal syndrome type 1, but the first step in a patient with SBP and rising creatinine is to treat the infection — the creatinine rise may be pre-renal from SBP, not yet HRS.
Increasing diuretics in a patient with active SBP and rising creatinine risks worsening renal function; norfloxacin is secondary prophylaxis after an episode of SBP, not a substitute for treatment.
clinicalPearl: In SBP, always give IV albumin alongside antibiotics — it prevents hepatorenal syndrome and reduces mortality, not just the antibiotic alone.
keyClue: Ascitic neutrophils 320/mm³ + fever + confusion + rising creatinine in known cirrhotic ascites.
commonExamTrap: Starting antibiotics without albumin, or misidentifying HRS before treating the precipitating SBP first.
missedConcept: SBP management requires both IV antibiotics and IV albumin; albumin is not optional — it prevents HRS development.
redFlagReason: SBP with rising creatinine and confusion in a cirrhotic patient carries high short-term mortality — delayed or inadequate treatment leads to hepatorenal syndrome and death.

subtopic: Coeliac disease — long-term follow-up and complications
clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman was diagnosed with coeliac disease 8 years ago. She has been on a strict gluten-free diet but attends follow-up reporting ongoing fatigue, loose stools, and a 4 kg weight loss over 6 months. Her repeat anti-tTG IgA antibody is undetectable. DEXA scan shows osteoporosis at the lumbar spine. Small bowel biopsy (under gluten-free diet) now shows persistent villous atrophy (Marsh grade 3). Which complication must be actively excluded in this setting?
options: Irritable bowel syndrome superimposed on coeliac disease
Refractory coeliac disease type II with risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL)
Helicobacter pylori-related peptic ulcer disease
Secondary lactose intolerance from residual villous atrophy
Microscopic colitis from long-term gluten-free diet
explanation: Refractory coeliac disease (RCD) is defined as persistent villous atrophy and symptoms despite strict adherence to a gluten-free diet for at least 12 months, with negative coeliac antibodies. RCD type II, characterised by an aberrant intraepithelial lymphocyte clone, carries a significant risk of progression to enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL), a rare but highly aggressive malignancy. This must be excluded urgently with immunophenotyping of small bowel biopsies.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IBS can coexist with coeliac disease, but persistent Marsh 3 histology on a strict gluten-free diet with weight loss demands exclusion of a sinister cause, not attribution to IBS.
Correct — negative anti-tTG + persistent Marsh 3 biopsy + ongoing weight loss = refractory coeliac disease type II must be excluded, with its associated risk of EATL.
H. pylori may cause dyspepsia but would not produce persistent small bowel villous atrophy on a gluten-free diet.
Lactose intolerance is a common early complication of coeliac disease but resolves with mucosal healing; it would not explain persistent Marsh 3 histology after 8 years on a gluten-free diet.
Microscopic colitis is an associated condition but would not cause persistent small bowel villous atrophy — it is a colonic diagnosis and does not produce villous atrophy.
clinicalPearl: Refractory coeliac disease type II carries significant risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma — any seropositive-negative persistent villous atrophy on GFD requires urgent immunophenotyping.
keyClue: Persistent Marsh 3 villous atrophy on GFD with undetectable anti-tTG + weight loss = refractory coeliac disease, not dietary non-compliance.
commonExamTrap: Assuming the patient is not following the diet because of ongoing symptoms, when in fact the negative antibody argues strongly for compliance — the histological persistence is the red flag.
missedConcept: Refractory coeliac disease type II and its association with EATL as a long-term complication in seropositive-negative persistent villous atrophy.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man underwent extensive small bowel resection (including 120 cm of distal ileum) following a mesenteric infarction 18 months ago. He has short bowel syndrome and is on total parenteral nutrition (TPN). He now presents with night blindness, dry skin, and a desquamating rash. Prothrombin time is prolonged. Which nutritional deficiency best explains this combination of findings?
options: Vitamin B12 deficiency
Zinc deficiency
Combined vitamin A and vitamin K deficiency
Iron deficiency anaemia
Folate deficiency
explanation: Following extensive ileal resection, fat-soluble vitamin absorption (A, D, E, K) is severely impaired due to loss of bile acid reabsorption, leading to bile acid depletion and fat malabsorption. Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness (nyctalopia) and dry/desquamating skin (phrynoderma). Vitamin K deficiency causes coagulopathy with prolonged prothrombin time. This clinical picture is therefore consistent with combined vitamin A and K deficiency from fat malabsorption in short bowel syndrome.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vitamin B12 deficiency is expected with terminal ileal resection but causes megaloblastic anaemia, subacute combined degeneration, and glossitis — not night blindness or coagulopathy.
Zinc deficiency causes dermatitis, alopecia, and diarrhoea but not specifically night blindness; coagulopathy is not a feature.
Correct — night blindness + dry desquamating skin = vitamin A deficiency; prolonged PT = vitamin K deficiency. Both are fat-soluble vitamins lost in fat malabsorption after extensive ileal resection.
Iron deficiency causes microcytic anaemia and fatigue but not night blindness or prolonged PT.
Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia but not the described combination of night blindness, rash, and coagulopathy.
clinicalPearl: After extensive ileal resection, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) must be monitored and supplemented — bile acid depletion impairs micelle formation and fat-soluble vitamin absorption.
keyClue: Night blindness + desquamating skin + prolonged PT after extensive ileal resection = vitamin A and K deficiency.
commonExamTrap: Students focus on B12 deficiency (expected after ileal resection) but miss the fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies causing the specific symptoms described.
missedConcept: Fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption after extensive ileal resection: vitamin A causes nyctalopia/skin changes; vitamin K causes coagulopathy.

subtopic: Monitoring after diagnosis of coeliac disease
clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with coeliac disease following investigation of iron-deficiency anaemia and chronic diarrhoea. Anti-tTG IgA is elevated at 15× upper limit of normal. Duodenal biopsy confirms Marsh 3b villous atrophy. She is commenced on a strict gluten-free diet (GFD). At her 12-month follow-up, which finding would best indicate a satisfactory response to treatment?
options: Normalisation of serum ferritin only, regardless of antibody titres
Reduction in anti-tTG IgA to within normal range, resolution of symptoms, and improved iron studies
Complete normalisation of duodenal biopsy within 3 months of starting GFD
Absence of gastrointestinal symptoms, with anti-tTG IgA still mildly elevated at 2× upper limit of normal
Normal bone density on DEXA scan performed at 12 months
explanation: The primary markers of response to a GFD in coeliac disease are: (1) symptomatic improvement, (2) normalisation of coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and (3) biochemical improvement (e.g. iron, folate). Antibody normalisation typically occurs within 6–12 months of strict dietary compliance. Histological recovery on biopsy often lags, taking up to 2–5 years. A mildly elevated anti-tTG at 12 months may indicate ongoing gluten exposure and requires dietary review.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ferritin improvement alone is insufficient — coeliac serology and symptom resolution are the primary response markers.
Correct — normalisation of anti-tTG IgA combined with symptom resolution and biochemical recovery is the standard definition of satisfactory GFD response at 12 months.
Complete histological normalisation within 3 months is not expected; mucosal recovery typically takes 1–3 years even with strict GFD.
A mildly elevated anti-tTG at 12 months despite claimed dietary compliance is not satisfactory — it suggests ongoing gluten ingestion and warrants careful dietary review.
DEXA scanning is appropriate at diagnosis to assess baseline bone density but normal DEXA at 12 months alone does not confirm mucosal or serological response to treatment.
clinicalPearl: Anti-tTG IgA is the primary non-invasive marker of GFD compliance and mucosal recovery in coeliac disease — it should normalise within 12 months on a strict GFD.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed coeliac disease, GFD started — response monitored by serology + symptoms at 12 months.
commonExamTrap: Expecting complete histological recovery at 12 months — mucosal healing lags behind serological and symptomatic recovery by years.
missedConcept: The hierarchy of coeliac disease response markers: serology and symptoms precede histological recovery; antibody persistence at 12 months implies ongoing gluten exposure.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man was admitted 12 days ago with severe gallstone pancreatitis. Initial CRP was 380 mg/L. He has been in the HDU, receiving IV fluids and analgesia, but over the past 48 hours has developed spiking fevers to 39.5°C, worsening abdominal pain, and deteriorating renal function. CT of the abdomen now shows extensive pancreatic necrosis involving > 50% of the gland with a gas bubble within the necrotic collection. WCC is 22 × 10⁹/L. Blood cultures are pending. Which is the most appropriate next step?
options: Increase IV fluid rate to 250 mL/hour and repeat CT in 7 days
Start prophylactic oral ciprofloxacin and observe for clinical improvement
Proceed immediately to urgent open pancreatic necrosectomy
Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g. meropenem) and arrange urgent CT-guided percutaneous drainage or endoscopic necrosectomy
Start total parenteral nutrition and defer any drainage procedure until day 21
explanation: Gas within a pancreatic necrotic collection on CT is pathognomonic of infected pancreatic necrosis — a condition with high mortality. The current standard of care is IV broad-spectrum antibiotics with good pancreatic penetration (e.g. carbapenems or quinolones) alongside a step-up approach to drainage: CT-guided percutaneous or endoscopic transgastric necrosectomy. Prophylactic antibiotics alone without drainage are insufficient once infected necrosis is confirmed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing fluids and delaying intervention is dangerous — gas in the necrosis is diagnostic of infection requiring active treatment, not watchful waiting.
Prophylactic oral antibiotics are not indicated in acute pancreatitis and are wholly inadequate once infected pancreatic necrosis is established.

Immediate open necrosectomy is associated with very high mortality in early infected necrosis — the step-up approach (percutaneous drainage → minimally invasive necrosectomy if needed) is preferred and evidence-based.
Correct — gas in pancreatic necrosis = infected pancreatic necrosis; this mandates IV antibiotics (with pancreatic penetration) and urgent step-up drainage (percutaneous or endoscopic).

Delaying drainage until day 21 without antibiotics is dangerous in established infected necrosis — the 'delay to drain' principle applies to elective necrosectomy for stable walled-off necrosis, not to actively infected necrosis with sepsis.
clinicalPearl: Gas within pancreatic necrosis on CT is diagnostic of infected pancreatic necrosis — start IV antibiotics with pancreatic penetration and arrange urgent step-up drainage immediately.
keyClue: Gas bubble within pancreatic necrotic collection = infected pancreatic necrosis, not sterile necrosis.
commonExamTrap: Choosing open necrosectomy as the immediate intervention — the step-up approach is now standard; open surgery reserved for failure of minimally invasive drainage.
missedConcept: Infected pancreatic necrosis management: the step-up approach and the significance of CT gas as a diagnostic indicator of infection.
redFlagReason: Infected pancreatic necrosis with sepsis (spiking fevers, high WCC, rising creatinine) carries mortality exceeding 30% without prompt treatment — delay is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of alcohol-related chronic pancreatitis presents with a 2-year history of offensive, pale, greasy stools that are difficult to flush, bloating, and a 9 kg weight loss. He has been abstinent from alcohol for 3 years. Stool elastase-1 is < 100 µg/g (severely low). Random blood glucose is 12.1 mmol/L. He is started on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT). Which important monitoring consideration is most critical on follow-up?
options: Repeat liver biopsy to assess alcoholic liver damage
Surveillance CT of the abdomen every 6 months indefinitely
Monitor for new-onset or worsening diabetes mellitus (type 3c) and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies
Screen for hereditary pancreatitis gene mutations
Arrange oesophagogastroduodenoscopy to assess for peptic ulcer disease from acid hypersecretion
explanation: Chronic pancreatitis causes progressive destruction of both exocrine and endocrine pancreatic tissue. Exocrine insufficiency leads to fat malabsorption and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K). Endocrine insufficiency causes type 3c diabetes (pancreatogenic), which is more difficult to manage than type 2 diabetes due to combined insulin and glucagon deficiency. The random glucose of 12.1 mmol/L in this patient already raises concern for established type 3c diabetes. Regular monitoring of glucose control and fat-soluble vitamin levels is essential.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Liver biopsy is not routinely indicated in chronic pancreatitis without clinical evidence of concurrent liver disease.
Surveillance CT is not routinely performed for chronic pancreatitis alone; it would be considered if pancreatic cancer is suspected, but CT every 6 months indefinitely is not standard practice.
Correct — type 3c diabetes and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies are the key long-term complications of chronic pancreatitis requiring active monitoring.

Hereditary pancreatitis gene mutation (PRSS1, SPINK1) screening is relevant in younger patients or those without a clear aetiology — not the primary follow-up concern in established alcohol-related chronic pancreatitis.
Acid hypersecretion is not a recognised complication of chronic pancreatitis; if anything, duodenal pH rises due to lack of bicarbonate from the pancreas, which can impair PERT efficacy.
clinicalPearl: Chronic pancreatitis causes both exocrine insufficiency (malabsorption, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency) and type 3c diabetes — both must be actively monitored and managed alongside PERT.
keyClue: Chronic pancreatitis + severely low stool elastase + random glucose 12.1 = exocrine AND endocrine insufficiency.
commonExamTrap: Treating only the steatorrhoea with PERT and missing the developing type 3c diabetes, which requires different insulin management than type 2 diabetes.
missedConcept: Type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes in chronic pancreatitis: different from type 2, associated with hypoglycaemia risk due to concurrent glucagon deficiency.

subtopic: Incidental pancreatic cysts and malignant transformation
clinicalStem: A 67-year-old woman has an incidental finding of a 2.8 cm cystic lesion in the pancreatic head on an abdominal MRI performed for unrelated back pain. She is asymptomatic, non-diabetic, and has no weight loss. Dedicated pancreatic protocol MRI shows the cyst communicates with the main pancreatic duct, and there is mild dilatation of the main pancreatic duct to 4.5 mm. CEA from endoscopic ultrasound fine-needle aspiration (EUS-FNA) fluid is 480 ng/mL. Which is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure the patient — pancreatic cysts are almost always benign in women
Annual surveillance MRI for 3 years, then discharge if stable
This likely represents an intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) with worrisome features; refer urgently for surgical evaluation
CT-guided biopsy of the cyst wall for tissue diagnosis before any further management
Start prophylactic octreotide and monitor CA 19-9 every 6 months
explanation: This cyst has multiple worrisome features for a branch-duct IPMN: communication with the main pancreatic duct, main duct dilatation (4.5 mm approaches high-risk threshold of 5 mm), size > 2 cm, and high CEA in cyst fluid (indicating mucinous content). Per international consensus guidelines (2017 Fukuoka, European guidelines), the combination of these features warrants urgent surgical referral rather than surveillance. IPMNs with main duct or mixed-type involvement carry significant risk of malignant transformation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pancreatic cysts are not universally benign — IPMNs with worrisome features carry real malignant potential and cannot be dismissed based on sex.

Annual surveillance may be appropriate for branch-duct IPMNs without worrisome features, but this patient has multiple concerning features (duct communication, duct dilatation, high CEA) that exceed the surveillance threshold.
Correct — this IPMN has worrisome features (size ≥ 2 cm, duct communication, near-threshold main duct dilatation, high CEA) and requires urgent surgical evaluation to determine resectability.
CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of pancreatic cysts is not standard practice — EUS-FNA with cyst fluid analysis has already been performed; the findings guide management.

Octreotide has no role in IPMN management; CA 19-9 monitoring is appropriate in established surveillance programmes but does not substitute for surgical evaluation when worrisome features are present.
clinicalPearl: Branch-duct IPMNs with worrisome features (size > 2 cm, main duct communication, duct dilatation, high CEA, mural nodule) require surgical evaluation rather than continued surveillance.
keyClue: Cyst communicating with main pancreatic duct + MPD dilatation 4.5 mm + high CEA = IPMN with worrisome features requiring surgery.
commonExamTrap: Defaulting to annual MRI surveillance for all pancreatic cysts, missing that worrisome features mandate surgical review rather than observation.
missedConcept: IPMN classification and risk stratification: the distinction between absolute high-risk stigmata (surgery) and worrisome features (EUS ± surgery) in Fukuoka/European guidelines.

subtopic: Post-hepatitis B acute infection — chronic carrier risk
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man from sub-Saharan Africa presents with acute icteric hepatitis. HBsAg is positive, IgM anti-HBc is positive, HBeAg is positive, and HBV DNA is 8 × 10⁷ IU/mL. He is not known to be a hepatitis B carrier. His ALT is 1100 U/L. He recovers over 6 weeks with supportive treatment. Which statement about his prognosis and follow-up is most accurate?
options: Positive HBsAg at presentation confirms he is already a chronic carrier; treat with tenofovir immediately
Repeat serology at 6 months is required — persistence of HBsAg beyond 6 months defines chronic infection, which carries risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma
Acute hepatitis B never progresses to chronic infection in adults and no follow-up serology is needed
IgM anti-HBc positivity proves immunity and no further monitoring is required
Commence pegylated interferon therapy during the acute episode to prevent chronicity
explanation: Acute hepatitis B is defined by IgM anti-HBc positivity. In immunocompetent adults, approximately 95% will clear the infection spontaneously; however, 5% will progress to chronic infection (defined as HBsAg persistence beyond 6 months). Chronic hepatitis B carries lifelong risks of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma, particularly in those with ongoing viral replication (HBeAg-positive, high HBV DNA). Repeat serology at 6 months is therefore essential.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IgM anti-HBc confirms this is acute infection, not necessarily chronic carriage; chronicity is only confirmed if HBsAg persists beyond 6 months.
Correct — HBsAg persisting beyond 6 months defines chronic hepatitis B infection, which requires lifelong surveillance for cirrhosis and HCC; follow-up serology at 6 months is mandatory.
While most immunocompetent adults clear acute HBV, approximately 5% progress to chronic infection — particularly those with perinatally or vertically acquired infection, or from endemic regions.
IgM anti-HBc indicates active acute infection, not immunity; anti-HBs is the marker of protective immunity following infection or vaccination.
Antiviral therapy is not recommended during uncomplicated acute hepatitis B; it is reserved for severe or fulminant acute disease.
clinicalPearl: After acute hepatitis B in an adult, repeat HBsAg at 6 months — persistence beyond 6 months defines chronic infection and mandates long-term HCC surveillance.
keyClue: IgM anti-HBc = acute hepatitis B; origin from HBV-endemic region = higher chronicity risk; follow-up serology at 6 months is mandatory.
commonExamTrap: Assuming acute hepatitis B always resolves in adults and no follow-up is needed — approximately 5% progress to chronicity with serious long-term complications.
missedConcept: Definition of chronic hepatitis B (HBsAg > 6 months) and the requirement for long-term HCC surveillance in chronic carriers.
